subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: In this unit your child learns about plants and how they grow. Plants are living things. They grow from tiny seeds into leafy green plants, just like the mango and banana plants near our homes. Your child will look at the parts of a plant, plant a seed, water it, and watch it grow over many days. This is slow, quiet, happy science. It teaches your child to look closely, to wait with patience, and to care for living things.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 1 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name the main parts of a plant: roots, stem, leaves and flower.
Say what each part of a plant does.
Say what a plant needs to grow: water, light and warmth.
Describe how a seed grows into a new plant.
Observe and record how a plant changes as it grows.
Care for a growing plant over time.

concepts.title: The Parts of a Plant
concepts.explanation: Start by looking at a real plant together. Take your child outside to a mango plant, a banana plant, or even a tuft of grass. Sit close and look at it slowly. A plant has different parts, and each part has a job, just like our body has parts with jobs - eyes to see, legs to walk. "Look at this plant. Allah made it with special parts. Let us find them together. Down here are the roots hiding in the soil. Up here is the stem holding the plant tall. These green parts are the leaves. And this pretty part is the…
concepts.example: Go slowly. Point to each part as you name it. Let your child say the word back to you.

concepts.title: Roots Hold and Drink
concepts.explanation: Plants are living things, and living things have needs. A plant cannot walk to find food or water like a goat or a chicken. It must get everything it needs from the place where it grows. There are four things every plant needs to grow strong and healthy. This is an easy idea for a young child, because your child has needs too. Talk about how your child needs food, water, and a warm safe home. A plant is the same. When you help your child see that plants are alive and have needs, you are teaching kindness and care…
concepts.example: Plants drink water through their roots. In the dry season we must water them so they do not wilt.

concepts.title: The Stem Carries Water
concepts.explanation: This is the favourite part. Your child will plant a real seed and watch it grow over many days. Every big plant, even a tall tree, started as a tiny seed. Inside each seed sleeps a baby plant, waiting for water and warmth to wake it up. Take a moment to fill your child with wonder before you begin. Hold up a single dry bean and say that a whole plant, taller than the cup, taller maybe than your child, is folded up asleep inside this one small seed. A seed can wait a very long time, dry and still, until it finds…
concepts.example: Break open one soaked bean gently and look inside. There is a tiny baby plant, and there is food packed around it to feed the baby plant until it can make its own food. A tough coat keeps it all safe. Show this to your…

activities.title: Look Inside a Flower
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of leaves and flowers around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Water One, Not the Other
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of what plants need to grow around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Make a Plant Diary
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of looking after a plant around your home or outside.
Change one thing, keep everything else the same, and record what happens.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: , you are not only teaching about plants. You are teaching your child to look closely at the world, to wait with patience, to care for living things, and to be thankful. These are gifts that will grow with your child for many years, long after this little bean plant is grown.

terms: Grow down in the soil. They drink water for the plant and hold it steady, like an anchor.
Stands up tall. It holds the plant up and carries water from the roots to the leaves.
The green parts. They catch the sunlight and make food for the plant.
The pretty, colourful part. It helps the plant make new seeds.
Plants drink water through their roots. In the dry season we must water them so they do not wilt.
Plants use light from the bright sun to make their food. Without light they turn pale and weak.
Seeds and plants like to be warm. The warm sun helps them wake up and grow.
Good soil holds the roots and gives the plant food from the ground.

vocabulary.meaning: Grow down in the soil. They drink water for the plant and hold it steady, like an anchor.
vocabulary.example: Say one job for each part (for example, roots drink water).

vocabulary.meaning: Stands up tall. It holds the plant up and carries water from the roots to the leaves.
vocabulary.example: Up here is the stem holding the plant tall.

vocabulary.meaning: The green parts. They catch the sunlight and make food for the plant.
vocabulary.example: It holds the plant up and carries water from the roots to the leaves.

vocabulary.meaning: The pretty, colourful part. It helps the plant make new seeds.
vocabulary.example: A real plant to look at - a mango plant, banana plant, a flower, or grass from outside.

vocabulary.meaning: Plants drink water through their roots. In the dry season we must water them so they do not wilt.
vocabulary.example: Your child will look at the parts of a plant, plant a seed, water it, and watch it grow over many days.

vocabulary.meaning: Plants use light from the bright sun to make their food. Without light they turn pale and weak.
vocabulary.example: Show care by watering a plant gently and keeping it in the light.

vocabulary.meaning: Seeds and plants like to be warm. The warm sun helps them wake up and grow.
vocabulary.example: A plant needs water to drink, light from the sun, warmth to keep cosy, and good soil to grow in.

vocabulary.meaning: Good soil holds the roots and gives the plant food from the ground.
vocabulary.example: Let your child touch the soil, hold the seed, and pour the water.

selfAssessment: I can name the main parts of a plant: roots, stem, leaves and flower.
I can say what each part of a plant does.
I can say what a plant needs to grow: water, light and warmth.
I can describe how a seed grows into a new plant.
I can observe and record how a plant changes as it grows.
I can care for a growing plant over time.
